episode_meta.title: Should AI have the 'right to read' news like humans do?

guests.name: Jeff Jarvis
guests.role: Director of the Tow Knight Center for Entrepreneurial Journalism; Professor
guests.organization: Craig Newmark Graduate School of Journalism at the City University of New York (CUNY)

use_cases.description: New York Times demonstrated GPT could verbatim reproduce entire articles from memory when prompted with sufficient paragraphs of an article, exposing a memorization flaw in LLMs
use_cases.outcome: Used as evidence in copyright lawsuit to show models can reproduce copyrighted content

use_cases.description: Microsoft Copilot used to chat and query specific New York Times news content, retrieving and producing content from the NYT website
use_cases.outcome: Cited as evidence of unauthorized content retrieval in NYT lawsuit

use_cases.description: A lawyer used ChatGPT to generate case citations for federal court, which turned out to be fabricated
use_cases.outcome: Fabricated citations submitted to court; highlighted LLM hallucination problem

use_cases.description: Proposal for a news API that would allow AI companies to query a database of news under negotiated contracts with limitations and payment terms
use_cases.outcome: Conceptual proposal for a licensing mechanism between news organizations and AI companies

use_cases.description: Amy Reinhardt's executive program project at CUNY exploring the creation of a news LLM
OpenAI negotiated licensing deals with Springer and the Associated Press for content use
use_cases.outcome: Licensing agreements established, though Jarvis argues this set precedent before principles were established

use_cases.description: Discussion of LLMs potentially extending literacy by enabling people intimidated by writing to speak to the machine and have it help articulate their stories
use_cases.outcome: Theoretical; raised concerns about code-switching to dominant language norms

key_claims.claim: AI training on news content is fair use because it is transformative — the model does not reproduce content, it learns statistical patterns to comprehend language
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

key_claims.claim: Newspapers were not included in copyright until 1909 in the US; the original 1790 US copyright only covered maps, books, and charts
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

key_claims.claim: The US Postal Act of 1792 enabled newspapers to send each other copies of their papers for free, intentionally creating a network for news sharing
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

key_claims.claim: Newspapers employed 'scissors editors' whose job was to cut content from other newspapers and reprint it — they wanted credit but it was free
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

key_claims.claim: The New York Times' demonstration of GPT reproducing articles verbatim was a 'parlor trick on a parlor trick' — they gave the model most of the article text, leaving only predictable words remaining
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

key_claims.claim: The biggest threat AI brings to the news business is that it commodifies content — making content is no longer special, but reporting, trust, authority, and service remain valuable
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

key_claims.claim: Sam Altman stated at Davos that OpenAI doesn't need to train on NYT data and expects models will learn from smaller amounts of higher quality data, similar to how a human can learn high school biology from one or two textbooks
key_claims.speaker: Nikita Roy (reporting Sam Altman's statement)

key_claims.claim: LLMs reflect back the 'publishing of the privileged' — our cliches, biases, and misapprehensions — because training data comes predominantly from those who had the privilege to publish
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

key_claims.claim: Models trained on AI-generated content degrade in quality — they 'go crazy a bit' and don't work as well, creating need for verified human-quality training data
key_claims.speaker: Nikita Roy

key_claims.claim: If we regulate and write laws for what generative AI does today, we would completely miss the path of technological development
key_claims.speaker: Nikita Roy (reporting Rebecca Tushnet's view)

key_claims.claim: Copyright was originally created (1710) for booksellers and publishers to create a marketplace for creativity as a tradable asset, not primarily to protect authors
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

key_claims.claim: News organizations lobbying politicians they should be independent of creates a conflict — they shouldn't be in a position to seek favors and be indebted to politicians
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

key_claims.claim: When radio emerged, newspapers tried to restrict them: radio could only do news twice a day, had to use wire services, couldn't have advertising, and couldn't cover events until 12 hours afterward
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

key_claims.claim: In Canada's C-18 legislation, when news organizations tried to get money from Google and Facebook for social media content, Facebook simply dropped news entirely
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

challenges.description: LLMs have no sense of fact — they don't lie but don't know what truth is (hallucination problem)
LLMs have a currency problem — models are only trained up to a certain date, lacking real-time information
Models can memorize and reproduce copyrighted content verbatim, which is a technical flaw that creates legal exposure
News organizations are lobbying politicians they should be independent of, compromising editorial independence
Training data predominantly reflects the 'publishing of the privileged,' embedding Western perspectives and excluding underrepresented communities and less digitized countries
Models trained on AI-generated output produce degraded results ('gray goo' / 'textpocalypse'), creating a need for verified human-quality data
LLMs extending literacy could force marginalized communities to 'code switch' to the white hegemony of language, as models mimic dominant publishing norms
Determining where liability lies for AI outputs — at the model level, the application level, or the user level — remains unresolved
News industry's protectionist response to AI mirrors its historical resistance to radio, TV, and internet — risking being left behind rather than adapting
Fair use boundaries are unclear — Larry Lessig described fair use as 'the right to hire a lawyer' — and will require litigation to clarify for AI
Acquisition of training data (e.g., Books3) raises ethical questions as creators of those datasets did not purchase every copy of the books included
OpenAI's licensing deals with Springer and AP may have set commercial precedent before legal and ethical principles were established

quotes.text: Does the machine have the same right to read and learn and use information as a journalist? Because every journalist on Earth reads other journalists' work and gets information from sources and does not pay them, learns from it and uses it and repurposes it in their journalism.
quotes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis
quotes.topic: AI right to read and fair use

quotes.text: The biggest threat that AI brings to our business is that it commodifies content. Now comes a machine that can make no end of content. And making content ain't so special. Writing isn't so special. Reporting is. Trust is, authority is, service is. That's where the value of journalism should be resident.
quotes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis
quotes.topic: Value of journalism in the AI era

quotes.text: All it's playing back to us is our cliches and our biases and our misapprehensions about the world as it is.
quotes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

quotes.text: News organizations are being quite short-sighted, as they have been in the past... when radio came along newspapers tried to keep them out of the news business. They made them sign an agreement that said that they would do no news or that they would do only news only twice a day.
quotes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis
quotes.topic: Historical pattern of news industry resisting new technology

quotes.text: If it is just data, then you can't own information. You can only own the treatment of it. I argue that news stories are just another form of data visualization. You could make a chart, you could make a box, you could make a story.
quotes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis
quotes.topic: Nature of news content as data

quotes.text: Rather than fighting at this point, as we are out of our field, we should be looking for ways to make it possible for AI companies to query and serve information from us and license for that purpose.
quotes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

recommendations.advice: Distinguish between AI training (fair use) and AI querying/output (licensable) — fight for licensing on the output side rather than trying to block all use of content
recommendations.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

recommendations.advice: Create a news API with keys distributed under contracts with limitations, enabling AI models to query a database of news under negotiated terms and payment
recommendations.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

recommendations.advice: Focus on labor laws and unfair competition laws rather than copyright law to address concerns about job displacement from generative AI
recommendations.speaker: Nikita Roy (citing Rebecca Tushnet)

recommendations.advice: News organizations should stop lobbying politicians they should be independent of — this creates conflicts of interest and indebtedness
recommendations.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

recommendations.advice: Invest in digitizing content from underrepresented regions (e.g., African continent) to address training data gaps rather than relying solely on existing Western-centric digital content
recommendations.speaker: Nikita Roy
recommendations.target_audience: AI companies and global news organizations

recommendations.advice: Negotiate constructively with AI companies rather than going to Congress and courts expecting a 'bucket of money' — AI is here and people will use it
recommendations.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

recommendations.advice: Rethink the 'romantic author' myth in copyright — recognize that creativity involves a chain of contributors and consider a 'credit rate' system that records the string of contributions to creative products
recommendations.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

recommendations.advice: AI liability should be placed at the application level rather than the model level — suing at the model level is like suing Gutenberg for everything ever printed
recommendations.speaker: Jeff Jarvis (reporting WEF San Francisco discussion)
